landing.description: Welcome to the CMS Made Simple Module Handbook. This resource is the definitive guide for developers who want to build, maintain, and distribute modules for ...

chapters.title: Intro

pages.title: Module Development
pages.description: CMS Made Simple is designed to be extended through modules. Whether you need a contact form, a photo gallery, an e-commerce system, or a custom admin tool — modules are the answer.

pages.title: What is a Module?
pages.description: A module is a self-contained package of PHP code that extends CMS Made Simple. Modules can create admin interfaces, display content on the frontend, manage d...

chapters.title: Module Basics

pages.title: Overview
pages.description: This chapter covers the fundamental building blocks of every CMSMS module: the required class methods that tell CMSMS about your module, the lifecycle files that handle installation and upgrades, the directory layout CMSMS expects, and the best practices that keep your code maintainable.

pages.title: Header Requirements
pages.description: Every CMSMS module is a PHP class that extends CMSModule. The base class provides a set of virtual methods that you override to tell CMSMS about your module ...

pages.description: CMSMS expects modules to follow a specific directory layout. Understanding this structure — and the API methods for referencing paths — is essential for writ...

pages.title: Including a Software License
pages.description: All CMSMS modules distributed through the Forge must be released under a GPL-compatible license. This is a requirement of the CMS Made Simple project itself,...

pages.title: Best Practices
pages.description: Following these conventions will make your module more secure, maintainable, and compatible with the CMSMS ecosystem. Many of these patterns are enforced by ...

chapters.title: Module Security

pages.title: Overview
pages.description: Security is not optional. Every module that accepts user input, displays data, or interacts with the database must implement proper security measures. A single vulnerability can compromise the entire site.

pages.title: Checking Permissions
pages.description: CMSMS has a built-in permission system that controls what admin users can do. Your module should create its own permissions during installation and check the...

pages.title: Nonces

pages.title: Securing (Sanitizing) Input
pages.description: Input sanitization is the process of cleaning data before you use it — removing or neutralizing anything that shouldn't be there. While validation checks whe...

chapters.title: Events

pages.title: Events
pages.description: Events are CMSMS's hook system — they allow modules to communicate with each other and with the core without creating tight dependencies. A module can send an event when something happens, and other modules can listen for that event and react.

pages.title: Sending Events
pages.description: Your module can define its own events that other modules or UDTs can listen for. This allows other developers to extend your module's behavior without modify...

pages.title: Handling Events
pages.description: Your module can listen for events sent by other modules or by the CMSMS core. When the event fires, CMSMS calls your module's DoEvent() method with the event...

pages.title: Core Events Reference
pages.description: The CMSMS core sends events at key points during content management, user administration, and page rendering. Your module can handle any of these events by r...

chapters.title: Admin Interface

pages.title: Admin Interface
pages.description: Most CMSMS modules include an admin panel — a set of screens within the CMSMS admin console where site administrators can manage the module's data and settings.

pages.title: Admin Actions
pages.description: In CMSMS, controllers are called "actions." Each action is a separate PHP file in your module directory that handles a specific request. When an admin action...

pages.title: Admin Tabs and Navigation
pages.description: CMSMS provides built-in support for tabbed interfaces in admin panels, control over where your module appears in the admin navigation, and the ability to add...

pages.title: Admin Forms and Smarty Plugins
pages.description: CMSMS provides a set of Smarty plugins specifically designed for building admin forms. These plugins handle action IDs, CSRF tokens, URL generation, and admi...

pages.title: AJAX in the Admin
pages.description: Many modern admin interfaces load data asynchronously using AJAX. CMSMS supports this through regular module actions that suppress the admin theme wrapper an...

chapters.title: Smarty Tags

pages.title: Smarty Tags
pages.description: Modules can register Smarty tags that content editors use in page templates and content blocks. This is how modules expose their frontend functionality — a calendar module provides a {cms_module module='Calendar'} tag, a navigation module provides {navigator}, and so on.

pages.title: Content Block Tags
pages.description: CMSMS page templates use content block tags like {content} to define editable regions. Modules can create custom content block types that appear as special f...

pages.title: Plugin Tags

pages.title: Tag Parameters
pages.description: When your module is called from a Smarty template, parameters can be passed to it. On the frontend, these parameters must be registered for security. On the ...

chapters.title: Preferences

pages.title: Preferences
pages.description: CMSMS provides three levels of preferences storage for modules: module preferences (scoped to your module), site preferences (global), and user preferences (per-user).

pages.title: Module Preferences API
pages.description: Module preferences are key-value pairs scoped to your module. They are the most common way to store module settings — items per page, default templates, feat...

chapters.title: Database Operations

pages.title: Database Operations
pages.description: CMSMS uses a modified version of adodb-lite for database abstraction. While only MySQL is supported, the abstraction layer provides parameterized queries, a data dictionary for schema management, and connection pooling.

pages.title: Using ADODB
pages.description: CMSMS creates a single database connection per request. You access it through the module's GetDb() method or the static \cmsutils::getdb() utility. All datab...

pages.title: Creating Tables
pages.description: CMSMS provides the ADODB DataDictionary class for creating, altering, and dropping database tables in a database-agnostic way. While CMSMS only supports MySQ...

pages.title: Schema Management
pages.description: As your module evolves, you'll need to add columns, create new tables, and migrate data. CMSMS handles this through the method.upgrade.php file. This page al...

chapters.title: Templates

pages.title: Templates
pages.description: CMSMS uses the Smarty template engine for all HTML output — both in the admin panel and on the frontend. Module templates are .tpl files stored in your module's templates/ directory.

pages.title: Smarty Templates
pages.description: Every module action that produces HTML output should do so through a Smarty template. Templates live in the templates/ directory of your module and are loade...

pages.title: Admin Templates
pages.description: Admin templates render the interface within the CMSMS admin console. They follow specific conventions for layout, forms, and navigation that ensure consisten...

pages.title: Frontend Templates
pages.description: Frontend templates render your module's output on the public website. Unlike admin templates, frontend templates have no prescribed style guide — the website...

pages.title: Design Manager Integration
pages.description: By default, module templates are .tpl files stored in the templates/ directory. This works well for development, but for modules intended for public distribu...

chapters.title: Users and Permissions

pages.title: Overview
pages.description: CMSMS has a built-in system of admin users, groups, and permissions. Modules interact with this system to control access to their features and, in some cases, to manage users programmatically.

pages.title: Roles and Permissions
pages.description: CMSMS uses a simple but effective permission model: admin users belong to groups, and permissions are assigned to groups. A user has a permission if any of t...

pages.title: Custom Permissions
pages.description: Most modules need at least one custom permission to control who can access their admin panel. Some modules define multiple permissions for different access l...

chapters.title: Internationalization

pages.title: Internationalization
pages.description: CMSMS modules can be translated into any language through language files and the CMSMS Translation Center. This chapter covers how to create language files, use translated strings, and prepare your module for localization.

pages.title: Language Files
pages.description: Language files store all human-readable strings used by your module. The base language file is lang/en_US.php — it serves as both the English translation and...

pages.title: Using Lang Strings
pages.description: The Lang() method is how you access translated strings throughout your module — in PHP action files, module class methods, and Smarty templates.

chapters.description: CMSMS modules can be translated into any language through language files and the CMSMS Translation Center. This chapter covers how to create language files, ...

chapters.title: Assets and Resources

pages.title: Assets and Resources
pages.description: Modules often need static assets — stylesheets, JavaScript files, images, and icons. This chapter covers where to put them, how to load them in admin and frontend contexts, and the conventions for module icons.

pages.title: Loading CSS and JavaScript
pages.description: Modules can include their own stylesheets and JavaScript files for both admin and frontend use. The approach differs between the two contexts.

pages.title: Images and Icons
pages.description: Modules use images for admin interface icons, frontend content, and Forge listing screenshots. Each has different conventions.

chapters.description: CMSMS 2.2 introduced an asynchronous job system that allows modules to schedule background tasks — one-time jobs or recurring cron jobs. Jobs are managed by ...

chapters.title: Guidelines

pages.title: Guidelines
pages.description: These guidelines define the standards every CMSMS module should meet before distribution. They are based on the 68 automated rules enforced by Module Check and CMSMS Scanner.

chapters.title: Tutorial

pages.title: Tutorial
pages.description: This tutorial walks you through building a complete CMSMS module from scratch. By the end, you will have a fully functional "Holidays" module with an admin CRUD interface, frontend views, pretty URLs, search integration, and AJAX.

pages.title: Getting Started
pages.description: In this first chapter, we create the module directory, the module class file with all the required virtual methods, and the language file.

pages.description: Our module needs a database table to store holidays and a permission to control admin access. We create these in the install routine and remove them in the u...

pages.title: The Model Class
pages.description: Before building the admin interface, we create a PHP class to represent a single holiday record. This keeps our data access code clean, reusable, and separat...

pages.title: Admin CRUD

pages.title: Frontend Views
pages.description: Now we add the frontend — a summary view that lists published holidays, and a detail view that shows a single holiday. This involves registering parameters, ...

pages.title: Pretty URLs and Routes

pages.title: Search, Lazy Loading, and AJAX
pages.description: In this final chapter, we add three enhancements: integration with the CMSMS Search module, lazy loading for the admin, and an AJAX-powered preview on the fr...

chapters.title: Forge

pages.title: Forge
pages.description: The CMSMS Forge (dev.cmsmadesimple.org) is the official repository for sharing CMSMS modules with the community. This chapter covers how to prepare, package, and submit your module.

pages.title: Submitting Your Module
pages.description: Before submitting your module to the Forge, ensure it meets the quality standards expected by the CMSMS community.

chapters.title: Developer Tools

pages.title: Developer Tools
pages.description: CMSMS module development is supported by diagnostic tools that scan your code for best practices, security issues, and structural compliance. Use these tools before submitting to the Forge.
